steps.prompt: How many finite verbs are in this sentence?

hints.one (est): 'Est' appears twice — once in the relative clause and once as the main verb
hints.three: There are only two verbs — both are 'est'
hints.none: Both instances of 'est' are finite verbs

hints.to eat: 'To eat' is 'edere/esse' (different verb) — 'est' here is from 'esse' (to be)

hints.edo, esse, edi, esum: These are the parts of 'edere' (to eat) — the infinitive 'esse' is identical but it's a different verb
hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.exeo, exire, exii, exitum: These are the parts of 'exire' (to go out)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'homines' — 'hominibus' is dative/ablative
hints.ablative: 'Hominibus' could be ablative, but here it is a dative of reference: 'lofty to men'
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'hominum' — 'hominibus' is dative

hints.accusative (neuter): 'Altum' could be accusative neuter, but here it is predicate nominative with 'est'
hints.ablative: Ablative neuter would be 'alto' — 'altum' is nominative/accusative
hints.vocative: Vocative would be the same form but one doesn't address an abstract quality here

hints.dative: Dative would be 'abominationi' — 'abominatio' is nominative predicate

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'abominationis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'abominationis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'abominationis' ends in -is

steps.prompt: What is the function of 'Quod' here?
steps.target: relative pronoun (nom./acc. neuter) introducing a relative clause
choices: relative pronoun (nom./acc. neuter) introducing a relative clause

hints.conjunction meaning 'because': 'Quod' can mean 'because' but here it is the subject of the relative clause — a pronoun
hints.conjunction meaning 'that': 'Quod' can introduce noun clauses, but here it is the neuter relative pronoun
hints.interrogative adjective: An interrogative 'quod' modifies a noun — here it stands alone as a pronoun

steps.prompt: What is the implied subject before 'abominatio est'?
steps.target: the whole relative clause 'quod hominibus altum est'
choices: the whole relative clause 'quod hominibus altum est'

hints.'hominibus' alone: 'Hominibus' is dative — it cannot be the nominative subject
hints.'altum' alone: 'Altum' is the predicate of the relative clause — the whole clause serves as subject
hints.'Quod' alone: 'Quod' begins the relative clause, but the entire clause functions as subject of 'abominatio est'

translation: What is exalted among men is an abomination
